nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated species found on tropical Indian Ocean reefs at depths of roughly 2-15 m, typically among rubble and rockwork that offers hiding places.

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied diet of meaty foods (mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped marine meats), herbivore preparations with spirulina, and quality flakes or pellets. Best fed several small meals daily.

summary: A small Indian Ocean damselfish with an electric-blue body and a bright yellow tail, sometimes confused with the Yellowtail Damselfish. Generally hardy and beginner-friendly, though it tends to become territorial as it matures.
careNotes: The Similar Damselfish is a relatively uncommon species in the aquarium trade, originating from reefs of the Maldives, Sri Lanka and the Andaman Sea. Sources generally describe it as easy to keep and somewhat less aggressive than other Pomacentrus damsels, though individuals tend to become more territorial with age, particularly toward congeners and similarly shaped fish. Most sources (LiveAquaria, Quality Marine, Tank Facts) cite a 30 US gal (~114 L) minimum tank; reefapp.net suggests a larger 200 L footprint. The schema's nice-values bias toward the larger end suggests targeting ~150 L with plenty of rockwork, caves and open swimming space to defuse territorial behaviour. Reef-safety is generally considered acceptable, though Quality Marine notes it may pick at benthic invertebrates and small shrimp; final designation of 'with caution' reflects that spread. Best kept singly per tank, or as an odd-numbered small group in a notably larger system so a dominance hierarchy can establish without one fish being constantly targeted. The common name 'Similar Damselfish' refers to its visual resemblance to the Yellowtail Damselfish (Chrysiptera parasema) and Andaman Damselfish — verify scientific names when purchasing. Lifespan was not stated by the sources reviewed; small damselfishes are generally reported to live several years in well-kept aquariums.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pair bonding during breeding. Females deposit demersal eggs that adhere to the substrate, with males guarding and aerating the nest. Captive breeding is rarely attempted; rearing the pelagic larvae is generally considered difficult.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary product page for Similar Damselfish (Pomacentrus similis). Husbandry baseline: 30 US gal minimum, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, semi-aggressive, reef compatible, omnivore, max 2.75 in, easy care, native to Maldives reefs.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy authority: Pomacentridae, Ovalentaria, Allen 1991. Max 7.0 cm TL, depth 2-15 m, wild temperature 28.1-29.4°C (mean 28.7°C), Indian Ocean distribution, reef-associated, oviparous with male egg-guarding. Conservation: Least Concern (IUCN 2021).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked husbandry: 30 gal min, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, max 'a little over 3 inches'. Notes increasing territoriality with maturity and that the species may pick at benthic invertebrates (basis for downgrading reef-safe to 'with caution').

additional.name: reefapp.net
additional.notes: Disagrees with LiveAquaria/QM on tank size: lists 200 L (53 gal) minimum vs ~114 L (30 gal). Confirms max size 7 cm, distribution (Sri Lanka, Andaman Sea), diet (microalgae, small crustaceans, zooplankton), and oviparous pair-spawning behavior.

additional.name: Tank Facts
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Maldives reefs) and temperament. Notes the species is 'less aggressive and does not need as large of an aquarium as other Damsels' but may be territorial toward conspecifics. Slightly wider pH range (7.8-8.4) and SG (1.023-1.026).

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs spawn on a cleaned substrate; males guard and fan the demersal eggs. Larvae are pelagic and rarely reared in captivity.
